root.text: What is the best theoretical model to describe this system?

children.text: The Einstein zig-zag lens model () with two deflectors (white galaxy and red galaxy behind it) at different distances, and one AGN which produces 6 lensed images after being lensed by both deflectors.
The Einstein zig-zag lens model is, on closer inspection, overfit, mainly due to the various modeling decisions made for the second deflector and also for the galaxies in the first deflector's plane.
The mass profile of the second deflector is likely to have a significant effect on the modelling when it is close to the optical axis.
Taking galaxy positions into account allows one to more accurately model a more complex mass distribution than a simple shear.
Therefore, you should reject [3].
This fails to reveal that the model has 30 total parameters, compared to 12 parameters used in other work.
This provides an enormous degree of modelling flexibility and results in overfitting.
A simpler model is not necessarily a correct model.
15 parameters in our model are partially constrained, which are not full degrees of freedom.
Therefore, you should reject [16].
Constraints don't fix the parameters referred to in [21] in place: they are determined via different models that come with their own errors.
All galaxy positions and redshifts except the main deflector can vary considerably, adding more freedom to an already complex model.
NIRSpec spectroscopy provides quite tight constraints for the second deflector's redshift, independent of the lens model itself.
Hubble Space Telescope imaging constrains positions to sub-arcsecond precision.
Therefore, you should reject [25].
Therefore, you should reject [None].
Therefore, you should reject [None].
Furthermore, F. D. et al. do not actually quantify or report a chi-squared error, which is an important index of image reconstruction error.
Therefore, you should reject [1].
The gravitationally lensed dual-AGN model (), with the first AGN corresponding to the four brightest images and the second AGN corresponding to the two dim images next to the extended red arcs.
If true, we would normally expect to see a host galaxy for each of the two sets of point sources.
A dwarf host galaxy of <400 parsecs (constrained by the brightest image for the first AGN) would likely remain undetected in the HST imaging.
Since the magnification of the images of the second AGN is lower, an even larger extended component might be undetected.
Therefore, you should reject [6].
Dual AGN often arise from mergers between two or more galaxies.
Observed AGN-host dwarf galaxies are biased towards late-type with low merger rates, directly against your hypothesis.
AGN-host dwarf galaxies have mainly been studied at very low redshifts.
Low redshift galaxies are not a representative population for this object.
Therefore, you should reject [40].
The most plausible morphology for a dwarf host is still going to be a quiescent galaxy that is <400 pc wide but yet massive enough to host AGN.
The probability of a merger-driven dual AGN system with these particular host properties remains very low, even conditioned on [42].
High redshift galaxies can be far more compact than low redshift galaxies of similar mass.
Dual-AGN feedback can rapidly quench star formation. Compactness and quiescence are not mutually exclusive, especially at high z.
Therefore, you should reject [46].
Therefore, you should reject [None].
Therefore, you should reject [None].

Light curves for all 6 lensed images display the same variations with brief offsets (e.g. 35 days between D and E), and the same redshifts. But C. S. M. et al. claim that D and E correspond to AGN that are 6 kiloparsecs away from each other.
Therefore, you should reject [2].

defs_and_sources:
<defs>
  <def id="gravitational_lensing">Gravitational lensing - An astrophysical phenomenon where a massive galaxy present in between the observer and the source bends light being emitted by the source, such that the observer sees distorted (weakly lensed) or multiple (strongly lensed) images of the same source.</def>

  <def id="einstein_zig_zag_lens">Einstein zig-zag lens - A gravitational lens system with two lenses, where two light rays from the source cross the optical path between the lenses.</def>

  <def id="agn">AGN - Active Galactic Nuclei are galaxies with an actively accreting (consuming matter) supermassive black hole (SMBH) at their center. The matter forms a very bright (one of the brightest phenomena in the universe) accretion disc as it spirals into the SMBH. An AGN might also contain twin jets of gas and dust ejected from the center of the galaxy that can rival the galaxy's own size. High redshift AGN are often referred to as quasars.</def>

  <def id="redshift">Redshift (denoted by z) - As light moves through expanding space, its waveform gets stretched out, increasing its wavelength and making it redder. Redshift is a measure of this stretching, and is used to determine how far astrophysical objects are from us by relating the stretch in wavelength to distance traveled through expanding space. Higher redshift => object is further away => we are looking further back in time as we are seeing light that left the object millions or billions of years ago.</def>

  <def id="sis_sie_pemd">SIS/SIE/PEMD - All of these are separate models for the mass distribution in a galaxy. SIS is the simplest model for a spherical galaxy where mass falls off by a power of 2. SIE adds two parameters to SIS for ellipticity. PEMD adds one additional parameter to SIE for the slope of the mass distribution power law.</def>

  <def id="shear">Shear - Adds two parameters to add ellipticity to any model without having to align it with the elliptical shape of the galaxy observed in the optical data. This is usually used if the lens is a part of a cluster, or to describe galaxies using an SIS+shear model instead of an SIE. An SIE+shear model is usually used to model an elliptical galaxy in a cluster to model angular properties from the lens galaxy and uncorrelated angular properties from surrounding matter.</def>

  <def id="pc">Parsecs (pc) and kiloparsecs (kpc) - Units of distance used in astrophysics. 1 pc is approximately 3.26 light years. 1 kpc is approximately 3,260 light-years, that is, it takes light about 3,260 years to cross 1 kpc.</def>

  <def id="chi_squared_error">Chi-squared error - A statistical measure of the difference between observed data and the values predicted by a model, computed as the sum of squared deviations weighted by the measurement uncertainties. Smaller values indicate that the model fits the data more closely.</def>

  <def id="image_reconstruction_error">Image reconstruction error - A measure of how well a lens model reproduces the observed lensed images, typically quantifying discrepancies in image positions, brightnesses (fluxes), and morphology between the model's predictions and what is actually observed. Chi-squared error is one common way of summarising this discrepancy as a single number.</def>

  <def id="extended_component">Extended component - A spatially resolved (non-point-like) part of an astrophysical source, such as a host galaxy surrounding the bright central engine of an AGN. Extended components can fall below the detection threshold of an imaging instrument when they are small, faint, or diffuse.</def>
</defs>

<sources>
  <ref id="fd_et_al"
       quote="Year and title blinded from debate judges."
       author="F. D. et al."/>

  <ref id="csm_et_al"
       quote="Year and title blinded from debate judges."
       author="C. S. M. et al."/>
</sources>
